speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Ruth, we were talking about the Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how long that lasted?

speaker: interviewee
text: It lasted from September '39 to '41-- in June-- June, I think-- when they got in the war-- the Russian with the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, when the Germans and the Russians were battling, how close where the battles to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not too bad. What I-- the Russian left quick. See, they were stationed not too far. And we was friendly with them. And I wish I had at that time sense and go with them. What happened, they came with the truck of the [INAUDIBLE]. And they say, come. They pleaded with me. And I said, I'm not leaving my father, my mother, my brother, my sister. What happens to them happen to me. And I no left.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, I felt bad-- sorry for myself. And my father hollered of me. He said, how come you no left? I said, dad, forget it. It's too late now. You see, what happening now? The German came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first week, the soldiers came to the house past the yard with the horses and was very-- you know, soldiers was going to the front, fighting. And they stopped-- got a break. And they came to yard to us. And one soldier came up to my father. He was a very nice guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, give me both your daughters-- I ship them to my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said, how can you do that? He said, don't worry. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: They go, but they are gentile girls, not Jewish. And my mother, I send them a letter, with them, and my mother keep them. You don't need to worry for that. And I say, no, nothing doing. Again, I don't want to go. And so he told my father, he said, sell the stuff. We have cars, we have horses. Try to sell how much you can. Accumulate money. Well, these Gestapos, when they come in, they like money. And sometime you can buy yourself out with the money. Not for long, but more you can longer be. I think, thanks to that guy, my father accumulated money, sold lots of stuff. And when they took me in concentration camp, I have enough money to pay-- as I-- we should come-- not him alone, like, but a dozen girls. We paid what we can go home nights to sleep. That be in the barracks. That was worth. We used to go every morning-- 5, 6 o'clock go home, and 6-- and 5 o'clock go back. At 6 o'clock, we need to be in camp. First, we used to wash the clothes from the people what was working there in the concentration camp. It was very bad. Where they made the camp was a monastery-- was preachers and nuns. And I don't know what happened to them that converts to a camp. And there was kettles-- big kettle, where they cooked for the pigs, for something still-- with wood under an oven. And we used to take the shirts, put the hot-- put water-- go to the pump-- carry buckets of water, throw it in that kettle, and throw in the clothes, put hot-- and the lice should come down. With spoons, we used to take out the shirts-- pick up with spoons. And after that, we washed it. Put with spoon. And with a spoon, we used to take out the lice in from the kettle. That's how bad it was. We used to work like that for six months, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: I need to go back a little bit. Can you tell me how long-- like, when the Germans first came to occupy you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --describe what changed, what changes there were in your life--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [INAUDIBLE], we was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and how long it was until you went to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first we changed with the-- it was the police-- was Ukrainian police first that made it. We have a station. Used to be Polish police first. And after that, the Russian it was. We have a big, nice, built station. And was after that, Ukrainian policemans. That the German made Ukrainian policemans. And we used to go, every day, helping cooking-- the lady will cook for them and cleaning. We used-- they made us for cleaning girls. And one time we used to have a big, tall guy who was a commander. He was bad. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. One guy, I remember the name-- was sadist. We used to call him sadist. He was one like that. He was short. He was, like, 5' 6'. The meanest guy of all. And one policeman used to work there-- or used to work for my father-- a herd driver. He used to bring the cattles with my father. He used to be the policeman there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to take care a lot of me there. [NON-ENGLISH] And dad no like that. He didn't let me do hard work, dirty work. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day the sadist, he saw him. He say, now, you go clean the toilets. Well, you be cleaning the toilets, we was two girls-- me and my sister-in-law. At that time she was not my sister-in-law. At that time she was, you know, a girl. And we were both working. I think very nice for you. And you make girls cleaning the toilets. Well, I used to have a big mouth. And I got hit twice [INAUDIBLE] for that with a big mouth. And I say, I go home, and I go milk the cow and bring you tonight the milk. Well, we used to get-- bring milk for the-- you know, the Jewish people, they need to bring milk for the police to cook-- you know, to use it. And I say, with my dirty hands, I will clean the toilet, go home, milk the cows, and bring you the milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he say to the cook, what the lady was cooking, he say, it's her day tonight? She say, yeah, her day is tonight. I say, you know, sadist, you're so stupid. Every time you make the girls cleaning, you make the first time me to clean while the-- the guy what knowed me, he was not around. And he figured he get revenge. But the nicer thing is, I said, why you not make the guys clean? The guys coming every day, clean the yard, chopping wood, you know what I mean? Make the guys clean that. That's not the girls' job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guys got mad of me, why I told them to do it. I say, it's easier for you guys than for us. And that happened. And I used to go that, like, for two months, like, three times a week. Well, it was more girls, you know. We used to whitewash the walls. We used to wash the clothes. We used to clean. We used to do a lots of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was this because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now, how did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- we was-- we knowed-- we lived there. And they know that we're Jewish. And we need to wear bands--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you had armbands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Armbands, with the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what would happen if you didn't wear the armband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time was a problem. That time, they give us hell. One time came the Gestapos, and I never wearing the band. I used to wear it in a jacket in my pocket. And when I saw them, I put it on. And he said, aha, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, that's right. And I walked away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were there any other Jewish regulations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish-- was nothing special. We need just to work and wear the band. And that's it there, home. After we went in concentration camp, we need to wear yellow stars sewed into our clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, when-- when the Germans occupied, how were the Jewish businesses affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. There was nothing doing no more. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father allowed to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to buy cattle, but the Germans bought them. You know what I mean? For half money, he used to lose his-- he quit that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father sold his cattle, was he selling it for Russian money? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, for German marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: German marks he got.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, at that time was already the marks. He sold it [INAUDIBLE] one cow. He sold the horse to another friend, you know what I mean? You used to-- he sold. One horse he left, and one cow he left to have the milk and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you tell me about the day that you had to go to the camp for the first time? Who came to tell you? Or, did they arrest you? Or, how did it work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they came to-- my father used to work of the committee with four other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they put committee for the Jews for the together work. And he was working with that. The Gestapo came. And he arranged-- I need girls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first two weeks, they we paid attention. And my father would say, when the Gestapo-- you go hide in neighbors-- you know, to sleep out. And I slept for two weeks when-- I was the older one. My sister was-- I was 19. My sister was 15. She was a child, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened, and he said to me, go to sleep of to neighbors. And I went to sleep with one friend for two weeks, in the stable. It was bad. I came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father say-- I said, I'm not going no more tonight. He said, why? I said, I'm not going. He said, be a bad night. I say, why? I think we can't wait longer with the Gestapos. I think you need to go in camp. We buy-- we think we bought the Gestapo. He sends you home, girls. I said, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: 3 o'clock, we got a knock on the door. The policeman came, one, and a Gestapo. And he say, you ready to go in camp. And we went. My father say, we-- I bring them, don't worry, six girls. There was six girls. We used to go every day to wash the clothes in camp for the concentration guys. The nicer thing happening in camp. One day was snowing very bad, wintertime. And we came a half hour late. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was '42. We came very late. And the Gestapo was waiting for us. And when we walked in in that room where we washed the clothes, we saw trouble. I said, something happen here that's awful. And he told us we should stay straight. We're getting shot a half hour late. He took out the gun-- put it to the heart to me. I was the first one. And he said to me, why are not screaming. That should help me. I say, you shoot me today, and you'll be suffering tomorrow. I think God put in my mouth what to say. I believe in that. He said, to suffer, I'll let you live-- all six. He was so mad, he walked out and shot twice outside, the gun. We thought that he shot somebody of the yard. He was-- he want kill somebody that day. That's how mad he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came home, we told my-- what happened. And my father say, that's no good, girls. What are we going to do now? I say, nothing. We need to get up-- go earlier when there's snow and it's hard to walk. It's a big walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what happened. Happened summer-- summertime was easier. But wintertime was hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: We was going-- after a while, we quit washing. He put us to work of the highway. By the [NON-ENGLISH] we used to work. We used to have a-- uh-- how you call that? Like-- stones. Like, uh-- Like a quarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: A stone quarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The diamond mints-- put diamonds? Diamond in. You know, the diamond-- tore apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not sure, but go  on and explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we used to throw it in the barrels. And all the Jews was working-- the concentration camp-- wheeled out to the highway. And after a while, that was not too good. We went. And with a hammer, we need to chip the stones, make from big stones little, tiny one. And we was working of the highway. This one Gestapo said to us, maybe we get-- it was summertime. It was so hot. Bucket of water and a cup, and give everybody a cold drink. And I was doing that-- carrying two buckets of water, helping that. I-- I was trying to get away from that. Suddenly, I was working for the highway. I got a toothache, very bad. And I was sitting and crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Gestapo came up and said, what happened? I said, I have a toothache. He went and pushed me. A stone hit my leg and tore apart, a point stone, torn apart.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took off my day shirt and try to blot it. I still have a mark here-- tied. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then one guy said to him, they a dentist. Tell him you have a toothache. He can pull it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, how can he pull out here? And he say, he'll pull out the tooth-- don't worry. And he went-- came the dentist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, open the mouth. Let me see which one. And I showed it  to him. He took out his pliers from the pocket and took and pulled out and showed me the tooth. That was so painful, like I no felt pains. Natural-- just pulled her out. And I came to the concentration camp-- went in the hospital. There was a hospital-- a doctor. And he put bandage of my leg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was not too bad. Yeah, bad-- not bad-- we no paid attention. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a while, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] staying in the line for dinner. That was [NON-ENGLISH]. We have [NON-ENGLISH] for dinner. In the morning, they give us 8 ounces bread and black coffee. And lunch was soup. In the nighttime was [NON-ENGLISH] once in a while. And came the Gestapo. He said, you, you, you going upstairs tomorrow, clean the apartments. I said, I'm not going cleaning apartments. And I said, I work by machine, outside. And I'm going to that machine. He went the whip, and whipped me over this shoulder. My arm-- I can't pick up-- it just was a big, fat bump. And he cracked my bone in the back. I still suffer till today with that. And the girls looked of me. They want kill me. There was 40 girls there together. They want kill me, for I opened my big mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went in the morning to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He give me-- this next few days, he say, how is good to work by the machine with your arm? I said, pretty good. [INAUDIBLE] He say, you're soldier. And you're bad. I said, I know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We was numb. We no feel pains. After was not enough, we came back in the concentration camp. We was already in the concentration camp very nice. He took us six girls for two weeks to get showers, cold showers. We need to take off our clothes and walk naked from the hall to the shower. We used to cover ourself, putting the hand on the bust, putting one hand to the front. One day, I say to the girls, I said, we can't help that. He'd beat us-- we'd be suffering like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walk out. You call us soldiers? We act like soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one girl say, you-- every time, you want to be a big shot. I say, no. I said, walk out straight, naked. He wants to watch us naked. He will watch us. We went to the shower. We took a cold shower. Till today, I cannot take a cold shower. When the cold water hits me, I freeze. Is-- after that, he never took us more. He got tired. And what my husband-- he was not that time my husband-- he got to whipped 25 whips in concentration camp. My boyfriend, he was pretty good there. He went every time to the city-- took out wagons-- people-- to deliver the stones. Called, you know, work that. That was not bad. Sometimes, once in a while, he brought a loaf of bread. No more. Sometime he brought something else to eat. That was not bad. But like that was tough till 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at that point, were you living in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was living that time in camp. But before, we was living in two camps. We have one main camp, and one was a smaller camp. We were kept us, like, about 75 people, separate. And I was there. In 1943, beginning March, my father and mother was home that time. Well, my father paid the Gestapo paper money to stay in home and buy cattles. And they got drunk. They took out my brother. He was 13 years old. And took him out to kill him. But what happened, he turned his head. A bullet-- they're called 'dumdum' bullet, what tears the meat out. He turned his head-- hit him in the shoulder, and he fell. And they walked into the-- the room. They killed my father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo. Well, my brother came in the concentration camp-- that private house where we was. That time was Jews lived before. And after he make camps for to keep extra better workers for him. He liked that, the Gestapo. And when we was there, my other room came running the girl to me. She say, your brother was shot. Go see him. And when I saw that, I almost fainted. But the nice thing happened that time. I need to go to [INAUDIBLE]. I have money. I need to go to [INAUDIBLE], get his wagon horse and go to the other camp, called [INAUDIBLE]. And my sister was there. And I said, I need a doctor to save our brother. He was shot. And I brought back the doctor. And my sister came along. And he bandaged him. I cannot keep him in that camp where I was. And then the first thing, I went home. And he hollered, mother and dad were shot. I said, I don't believe you. I need to go see it. About 1 o'clock in the night-- was in March, was cold. And I went home, like, 5 miles. And I went. And the windows was covered with a blanket where my mother lived. I went with my whole strength-- made a fist-- hit that window and grabbed the blanket. And I found my mother and father. He had catched me-- a boy. He watched it. He knowed I come. He was Ukrainian. He was 6' 4' old-- tall.

speaker: interviewee
text: He catched me. And he said, don't you dare go in in the room. I said, I want to see my father and mother. He said, now, you want to be killed, too? They are maniacs tonight, the Germans. I said, what happened here? He said, they got drunk, very bad. And I have a feeling you come. When we no find your brother outside that, we figured maybe he was not killed, he go see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- he carried me back to the camp of his back. I screamed. I hollered. He said, nothing doing. You need to live for your brother and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So he gave you, really, some incentive to live. He was really telling you that, in spite of--

speaker: interviewee
text: You need to live. And you'll live through that. I said, Peter, I cannot do it no more. He said, oh, yeah. I say, you know what? Who needs to live and suffer? And I came back, and I told my sister what happened. And my brother knowed. He said it right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, you know what, Aaron? I need to take you. I need to get a release for the boys. I need to cover me for the Gestapo to take you in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told the guy who had picked up the doctor. He took him back. I said, 6 o'clock in the morning, I want you back. And we taking my brother to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what happen when they catch us? I said, I don't want to be killed. I say, nobody catch us. Don't worry. And which ghetto was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Peremyshlyany ghetto-- was in Europe-- Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was it from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, was a-- like, from here, twice Fox Chapel. More, maybe more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a half hour, an hour, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: An hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: An hour away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took an hour away. And we went there. And I took my-- And we went. Took us longer, well, we went and different streets, the Gestapo should not catch us. No, the fields-- we went-- don't ask. I came in in the ghetto, and I brought my brother. The ghetto-- the doors are closed. But came up a policeman. Was my luck, that time, too. He knowed me, you know, from before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he say to me, what are you doing here? I say I brought my brother. My parents were shot. I need to take him in hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, come on, I carry him. He carried him. My brother was 13-- 13 years old. And he carried him to the hospital. And I left him in the hospital. They bandaged him. And I said, I'll try to be here. I don't know when. But my cousins was in ghetto there [INAUDIBLE], and while the mother they took in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Why don't we stop, and you'll tell us a little bit more in a minute.
